macro_segments.title: Introduction to Perek Kol Habasar and the Prohibition of Meat and Milk

micro_segments.title: Opening of the new perek on meat and milk; overview of three-week coverage
Mishnah: prohibition of cooking all meat with milk, with exceptions of fish and grasshoppers
Rabbinic extensions: prohibition from table and vows indicating fish and grasshoppers are not meat
Interpretation of vows and the vernacular definition of meat; hints at why birds are rabbinically included

macro_segments.title: Conceptual Issues: Biblical vs. Rabbinic Prohibitions and Safeguards

micro_segments.title: Discussion of why birds are extended rabbinically despite lacking mother's milk; connection to vernacular understanding of meat
Two areas of rabbinic extension: prohibited actions and prohibited categories
Structure of earlier mishnayot contrasted with current perek on meat and milk

macro_segments.title: Gemara Analysis: Are Birds Biblically or Rabbinically Prohibited?

micro_segments.title: Gemara assumes birds biblically prohibited from meat and milk based on safeguards in the mishnah
Tosafot reasoning: extensive rabbinic safeguards suggest underlying biblical concern
Comparison to Rabbi Akiva's position that birds are not included in meat and milk
Gemara applies Rabbi Akiva's vow principle: if a messenger must check, the item is included in category
Analysis of vegetables vow example and Rabbi Akiva's ruling on peripheral categories
Gemara notes inconsistency: mishnah's inclusion of birds suggests both biblical status and vow-principle agreement with Rabbi Akiva

macro_segments.title: Resolving the Contradiction: Mixed Attribution to Rabbi Yehuda Hanasi

micro_segments.title: Rav Yosef's solution: Rabbi Yehuda Hanasi is free to choose different authorities for different topics
Rabbi Yehuda Hanasi holds like Rabbi Akiva for vows but like the Rabbis for meat and milk
Discussion of the questionable nature of any tannaim actually holding birds are biblically prohibited

macro_segments.title: Rav Ashi's Alternative Interpretation: Mixed Biblical and Rabbinic Meat

micro_segments.title: Rav Ashi reinterprets entire mishnah as Rabbi Akiva's position with all meat prohibited but only some biblically
Birds are rabbinically prohibited while fish and grasshoppers are neither biblical nor rabbinic

macro_segments.title: The Problem of Multiple Safeguards: Why Safeguard Against Rabbinic Violations?

micro_segments.title: Rav Yosef's argument: if birds are only rabbinic, why make additional safeguard against table proximity?
Definition of safeguard on safeguard and when such doubling is permissible
Tosafot's discussion of when gemara permits or forbids multiple safeguards
Gemara questions whether prohibiting table proximity is justified safeguard doubling

macro_segments.title: Abaye's Response: The Slippery Slope of Meat and Milk

micro_segments.title: Abaye distinguishes challah case where safeguard is not feasible from meat and milk where slope is real

macro_segments.title: Extended Discussion: Safeguards, Definitions, and the Nature of Eating Together

micro_segments.title: Student questions about practical understanding and distinction between chicken and meat
Discussion of cultural complexity: are people expected to know all distinctions in kashrus?

macro_segments.title: Abaye's Response: The Slippery Slope of Meat and Milk (continued)

micro_segments.title: Challenger to Rav: even cold food at table cannot lead to biblical violation of cooking together
Gemara's response: safeguard against hot pot brought directly to table leading to cheese-dropping scenario
Discussion of kli sheni and whether second vessels can cook; comparison to Shabbat stringencies

macro_segments.title: Mishnah on Birds and Cheese: Beis Shammai and Beis Hillel Dispute

micro_segments.title: Mishnah: Beis Shammai allows birds and cheese at table but prohibits eating; Beis Hillel prohibits both
Debate between two positions indicates birds are rabbinic since biblical matters are not debated
Rabbi Yosi's statement that this is Beis Shammai leniency and Beis Hillel stringency

macro_segments.title: Extended Discussion: Safeguards, Definitions, and the Nature of Eating Together (continued)

micro_segments.title: Suggested distinction between eating table and preparation table; when consciousness affects the safeguard

macro_segments.title: Mishnah on Birds and Cheese: Beis Shammai and Beis Hillel Dispute (continued)

micro_segments.title: Gemara clarifies: distinction applies to eating table; preparation table has different rules
Rashi and alternative explanations of why preparation table is treated more leniently

macro_segments.title: Extended Discussion: Safeguards, Definitions, and the Nature of Eating Together (continued) (continued)

micro_segments.title: Lecturer's interpretive framework: extensions are rabbinic redefinitions of eating together, not just safeguards

macro_segments.title: Baraita on Eating Meat After Cheese: The Role of Hand Washing and Mouth Cleaning

micro_segments.title: Baraita of Igra: bird and cheese can be eaten one after another without hand or mouth washing
Story of Rabbi Yitzchak visiting Rav Ashi: ate cheese then meat without washing hands
Rabbi Yitzchak's justification: can see hands are clean during daytime; implies different concerns at night
Baraita: Beis Shammai says wipe mouth; Beis Hillel says rinse mouth
Gemara interpretation: both sides agree you can do either; just one or the other is needed

macro_segments.title: Detailed Rules for Mouth Cleaning: Types of Bread and Foods

micro_segments.title: Rav Zeira: wipe mouth with bread; wheat bread only, must be cold and soft
Qualifications on bread type: not barley, not hot, not hard; only wheat bread works
Conclusion: in practice any food is acceptable except flour, dates, and vegetables

macro_segments.title: Summary and Forward Look: Current State of Meat-Milk Prohibitions

micro_segments.title: Summary of prohibitions so far: table separation, one-after-another eating with hand/mouth cleaning
Note that all scenarios discussed assume milk first, then meat; order question deferred to next session

topical_tags.term: Rabbi Akiva vs Tannaim on Meat Definition
Challah Outside Land and Rabbinic Extension
Beis Shammai vs Beis Hillel on Meat and Milk
